column2: Today, chemists often use standard computer calculations to predict molecular shapes, reaction energies, energy barriers, and spectra.

Most of those quantum-chemistry calculations use density functional theory, or DFT, together with atomic-orbital basis sets, which are mathematical building blocks for describing electrons around atoms.

This article gives practical advice for running DFT calculations in three parts: first, it introduces a decision tree for choosing a computational setup that matches the real experiment as closely as possible.
Second, it gives a recommendation table for choosing the DFT functional and basis set that fit the specific job.
A central theme is balancing three needs at once: accuracy, reliability, and reasonable computational cost, often by combining cheaper and more expensive calculation levels.
Finally, the paper works through representative examples to show how the recommended protocols behave and how different technical choices change the answer.
Chemistry and synthesis help address major problems such as energy storage, sustainable food production, and affordable medicines.
In each of those areas, researchers need to design molecules and materials rationally, such as catalysts, battery electrolytes, electronic materials, and drugs.
To do that well, chemists must understand atoms and electrons, using experiments, spectroscopy, and quantum-chemistry calculations together.
Computational chemistry, especially Kohn-Sham DFT, has become a third major tool alongside making molecules and measuring them experimentally.
The authors argue that DFT is important because it often gives a lot of chemical insight for the amount of time, money, and effort spent.
In principle, DFT starts from fundamental quantum mechanics, but in practice it uses empirical approximations to handle the many-electron problem behind chemistry, biology, and physics.

When DFT is paired with models for nuclear motion, such as molecular dynamics or harmonic potential-energy approximations, it can answer many chemistry and physics questions accurately enough to be useful.
The deeper theory of DFT has already been covered in a separate open review, so this paper points readers there for detailed theoretical issues and stays focused on practical use.
For broader challenges in theoretical and computational chemistry, the authors direct readers to references [2] and [3].

Compared with other methods, DFT is a middle ground: it is slower but more dependable than semi-empirical quantum mechanics, and faster but usually less rigorous than wavefunction methods such as coupled-cluster theory, as sketched in Figure 1.
Efficient coupled-cluster variants, including DLPNO-CCSD(T), can sometimes compete with high-level DFT, but this paper does not focus on them.
DFT can also save researcher time and computer power.
Another advantage is robustness: DFT usually does not fail catastrophically, even for difficult molecules or unusual chemistry.
Semi-empirical and other more empirical methods need more careful checking because they are more likely to give misleading results.
Because of this reliability, non-specialists can often use DFT as a practical black-box tool for chemical problems.
Typical uses include checking whether proposed structures and mechanisms make sense, or plotting frontier orbitals and HOMO/LUMO energies to interpret electrochemistry in materials science.
Even so, choosing a method that is affordable, reliable, and accurate remains hard, even for experienced computational chemists.
One reason is that recent years have produced a huge number of possible method combinations.
Even the basic pairing of a density functional with an atomic-orbital basis set can involve hundreds or thousands of options in common software.
For tiny molecules, using a very expensive method may be acceptable, but systems with 50-100 atoms or many low-energy shapes force researchers to make careful cost-versus-accuracy compromises.
Some quantum-chemistry programs also still offer outdated defaults, which can lead inexperienced users toward methods that are no longer recommended.

A key example is B3LYP/6-31G*, which remains common even though it can perform poorly because it misses London dispersion and suffers from basis-set superposition error; awareness of these problems has spread slowly beyond theory specialists.
Tutorials that explain these weaknesses and show better choices, such as work by Peverati and Morgante or by Mobley, are therefore useful for the field.
Over the past 10-20 years, better dispersion corrections and BSSE corrections have made routine B3LYP/6-31G* calculations obsolete.
Researchers now have alternatives that are often more accurate, more robust, and sometimes cheaper, including composite methods such as B3LYP-3c, r2SCAN-3c, B3LYP-D3DCP, and B97M-V/def2-SVPD/DFT-C.
These newer methods are designed to remove the systematic problems of B3LYP/6-31G* without making the calculation more expensive.
Figure 1 compares the computational cost of the main approaches, and Sections 4.2 and 4.3 show concrete failures of B3LYP/6-31G* and ways to avoid them.
The paper's main aim is to introduce these improved methods to chemists who are interested but not specialists, including readers with some theory background.
It gives best-practice advice for common tasks such as finding structures and conformer ensembles, calculating reaction energies and barriers, estimating free energies, and treating solvation.
It also briefly explains how reliable DFT calculations can be embedded in multi-level workflows to study solvated and temperature-averaged sets of conformers, protomers, or tautomers.
Because quantum chemistry is broad, the paper mostly leaves aside topics such as theoretical spectroscopy, excited states, periodic materials, and heavy-element chemistry.
It also does not cover detailed analyses of atom and fragment interactions, such as energy decomposition analysis or natural bonding orbital analysis.
The recommendations draw on more than 25 years of work in DFT, functional development, thermochemical benchmarks, and many collaborative chemistry applications.
Most advice is evidence-based, relying on large benchmark comparisons between approximate DFT, experiment, and high-accuracy coupled-cluster calculations.
Some judgments, however, are hard to prove cleanly with statistics because method performance can depend on the model, basis set, functional, and chemical case.
For that reason, the guidance also reflects the authors' practical experience.
The authors value robustness more than best-case benchmark ranking, because in predictive chemistry avoiding large unexpected errors matters more than being exact to the final kcal mol-1.

The authors stress that standard DFT still uses approximations, so even a robust functional cannot guarantee perfect answers; they also note that in computational chemistry, thermochemistry commonly includes reaction barriers as well as reaction energies.
Choosing an appropriate theoretical method is essential for describing a chemical system accurately.
That choice includes both the electronic-structure method and the model system used to represent the real physical and chemical situation.
In practice, this requires several connected decisions.
Figure 2 gives a flowchart for those decisions across many common molecular quantum-chemistry applications.
The first major question is whether ordinary DFT is suitable: is one main electronic configuration enough, or does the system need several configurations and therefore a multireference treatment?
Fortunately, most routine cases belong to the single-reference category.
Those systems can usually be described with the DFT methods discussed in the paper.
This is especially true for most diamagnetic closed-shell organic molecules, which normally have a single-reference electronic structure.
Exceptions such as biradicals can have nearby triplet states, and an unrestricted broken-symmetry DFT calculation can help detect that problem.
Multireference behavior is more likely for radicals, small HOMO-LUMO gap systems, open-shell bond-breaking transition states, and transition-metal complexes.
Partly filled 3d transition metals are especially prone to this because their ligand fields can create several close electronic arrangements.
In such cases, several nearly equal electronic configurations with different orbital occupations may contribute, making the electronic structure complicated.
Therefore, researchers should check early whether these systems are multireference cases.
Although a full test is difficult, simple warning signs can often give a useful first assessment.
One warning sign is a very small HOMO-LUMO gap, below about 0.5-1 eV in a test GGA calculation, together with unusually slow self-consistent-field convergence.
This is an extraction artifact from the journal header, not scientific content to interpret.
The broken text continues the previous warning: poor convergence can indicate unusual electronic complexity.
If those rough signs appear, researchers should use more advanced checks.
Most rigorous checks use wavefunction theory, which makes them practical mainly for small molecules.
A more accessible DFT-based option is the fractional-occupation-number-weighted density, or FOD, estimate.
In that approach, researchers slightly raise the electronic temperature so low-lying problematic electronic states become populated and visible.
If a system shows clear multireference character, standard DFT is not recommended and a specialist in multireference methods should be consulted.
Reference [44] discusses the distinction between true multireference cases and related multi-determinant behavior in low-spin open-shell systems.

The practical recommendations are to check HOMO-LUMO gaps or FOD, be extra cautious with open-shell systems, and avoid ordinary single-reference DFT for multireference cases; the extracted sentence also contains the start of the next solvation discussion.
Explicitly modeling individual solvent molecules can make the energy landscape very crowded with local minima, so it should be used cautiously.
Ignoring solvent effects, especially for polar or charged molecules, can badly distort thermochemical results and can even give the wrong electronic structure, for example in zwitterions.
The next basic question is the molecule's physical environment: is it isolated, in a solid, in solution, or part of a mixture?
Nearby molecules in a solid or solvent can strongly change the structure and properties of the system being studied.
For condensed-phase chemistry, researchers should therefore include an appropriate solvent model.
In DFT, the usual shortcut is a continuum solvation model, which represents the solvent as an averaged surrounding field in the Hamiltonian rather than as individual molecules.
In other words, the calculation includes solvent effects without placing actual solvent molecules around the solute.
Common continuum models include CPCM, SMD, COSMO, COSMO-RS, and DCOSMO-RS, and they differ in what physical solvent effects they include.

CPCM and COSMO mainly treat electrostatics, so they miss cavity-formation costs and attractive van der Waals solvent interactions, which can cause serious errors when solvent-exposed surface area changes.
SMD, COSMO-RS, and DCOSMO-RS include those extra solvent contributions, so the authors recommend them.
The detailed example appears in Section 4.2.
COSMO-RS cannot be used directly for geometry optimizations or frequency calculations, so DCOSMO-RS must replace it for those steps.

Other implicit-solvent options include GBSA and ALPB, which can also be used with semi-empirical and force-field methods; however, some problems still require actual solvent molecules placed at the most important binding positions, especially when those solvent molecules participate strongly in the chemistry.
A cheap test calculation on a representative model can help decide whether explicit solvent molecules matter.
Explicit solvent is difficult because calculated properties may keep changing as more solvent molecules are added.

The authors recommend matching the modeled aggregation state to the experiment, using good implicit solvent models for solution chemistry, treating highly charged systems cautiously, and adding explicit solvent only when necessary.

CREST followed by CENSO is a useful semi-automated way to search molecular shapes and rank conformer-rotamer ensembles, meaning sets of low-energy arrangements from bond rotations and conformational changes.
CREST also reports a flexibility index that can warn how flexible a molecule is.
Other conformer-generation tools exist, including Molassembler, ConfGen, MS-DOCK, Frog2, OMEGA, RDKit, and related programs.

The practical advice is to search conformational space, use automated tools such as CREST, verify the lowest-energy conformer, and consider Boltzmann averaging when properties depend on multiple shapes.
One of the most important DFT choices is the exchange-correlation functional, usually shortened to functional.
The functional is meant to capture complicated electron correlation and exchange effects inside a usable expression for electronic energy and potential.
Hundreds of functionals exist, and they differ in philosophy, intended use, amount of fitting, and overall reliability [16,76].
This paper does not review all functional theory; it focuses on which functionals are useful for thermochemical applications.

Perdew's Jacob's ladder groups functionals by the physical information they use, from simple density information up to ingredients such as Fock exchange and MP2-like virtual-orbital correlation, with higher rungs generally expected to be more accurate.
The main practical categories, from cheaper to more sophisticated, are GGA, meta-GGA, hybrid, and double-hybrid functionals.
Higher rungs often improve accuracy, but functionals on the same rung can still perform very differently.
Therefore, benchmark studies for the specific property of interest are essential.
Even so, Jacob's ladder gives a rough sense of which systematic errors a functional may have.
It also helps classify functionals by computational cost.
The most important cost distinction is whether the functional includes Fock exchange, also called exact or nonlocal exchange.
Functionals up through rung 3 are local or semi-local and do not use Fock exchange, while hybrid and double-hybrid functionals on rungs 4 and 5 do use it.
Because Fock exchange is expensive to compute, semi-local functionals are usually faster than hybrid or double-hybrid methods.
Two major DFT approximation errors are self-interaction error and the missing long-range correlation responsible for London dispersion.

Missing dispersion can now be fixed with corrections such as D4, D3, or VV10, and the authors argue that essentially every DFT calculation should include such a correction unless dispersion itself is being studied.
Section 3.7 and example 4.2 give details and numerical examples.

Dispersion strongly affects energies but seems to have little effect on electron density, while self-interaction error comes from an electron incorrectly interacting with itself because approximate exchange-correlation does not cancel the electron's own Coulomb repulsion perfectly.
Hartree-Fock avoids self-interaction error because its exact exchange term mathematically cancels an electron's self-Coulomb interaction.
In DFT, self-interaction error often spreads electron density too much and makes delocalized electronic states artificially stable, with stretched H2+ as a classic example.
All semi-local GGA and meta-GGA functionals, such as PBE and r2SCAN, contain this error.
Hybrid functionals reduce self-interaction by replacing part of approximate DFT exchange with Fock exchange, for example 20% in B3LYP and 25% in PBE0.
That replacement helps, but it does not remove the error completely.
So even functionals containing Fock exchange can still suffer from self-interaction error.
Using 100% Fock exchange is not a simple fix because it usually performs poorly overall.
As a rough guide, 5-25% Fock exchange is small, 25-40% is moderate, and more than 40% is high.

Range-separated hybrids vary the Fock-exchange amount with electron-electron distance; more generally, self-interaction is part of the broader delocalization-error problem, and double-hybrid functionals add a wavefunction-theory correction at the highest rung.

Double hybrids usually add perturbation-theory correlation such as MP2 or a DFT variant, and their high Fock-exchange content makes them more resistant to self-interaction error while the correlation term compensates for problems caused by that much exchange.
The tradeoff is that double hybrids cost more and are not universally applicable.
For example, MP2-based double hybrids can struggle with small-gap systems, so those cases require caution.
Hybrid and double-hybrid functionals can be more accurate, but their cost and scaling become a serious issue for large molecules.
With density fitting, GGA and meta-GGA treatments formally scale roughly as N^3 with system size.
For local GGA or meta-GGA functionals, density fitting can make calculations about 5-30 times faster, depending on the molecule and basis set.
Hybrid methods scale roughly as N^4, and MP2-based double hybrids roughly as N^5.
Because of that scaling, higher-rung functionals can become impractical for systems larger than about 200-300 atoms on ordinary hardware.
The main reason is that density fitting does not speed up Fock exchange as effectively, so exchange dominates hybrid calculation time.
Semi-numerical exchange methods such as COSX can speed hybrid calculations by about 3-10 times for triple-zeta or larger basis sets when combined with density fitting.
The authors strongly recommend using RI and COSX when the software offers them, such as RIJCOSX in ORCA or SENEX in TURBOMOLE.
Density fitting also accelerates the MP2 part of double hybrids, making them only somewhat more expensive than hybrids, so this safe approximation is strongly recommended.
Besides reading benchmark studies, users should compare a few suitable functionals on a representative model to check whether conclusions are consistent.
Those test functionals should come from classes appropriate for the problem, such as range-separated hybrids or double hybrids for barrier heights.
This consistency check is especially important when the studied system differs strongly from the systems used in published benchmarks.

Minnesota functionals, developed by the Truhlar group since 2005, are widely used and can perform very well on main-group benchmarks such as GMTKN55; their names encode year and purpose, such as HF, 2X, or L.

They improved on B3LYP and solve some difficult correlation problems, but they can be sensitive to integration grids and basis sets, can create rough potential-energy surfaces, and can behave very differently across chemical systems.

Their strong performance for normal main-group chemistry comes partly from extensive parameter fitting, which can fail for unusual systems and transition-metal chemistry; performance also depends on how much Fock exchange each Minnesota functional uses.
Reference [16] discusses that dependence in more detail.
A third caution is that Minnesota functionals can be problematic for noncovalent interactions.

Although they include some dispersion-like behavior and can work near equilibrium distances, they do not reproduce the correct long-range London dispersion limit for large separations, which matters for big molecules, solids, and liquids.

Sometimes a separate dispersion correction must be added to Minnesota functionals, but that can over-bind in other cases; overall, choose functionals for the system and task rather than popularity, and include dispersion corrections as standard.

Hybrids are often more accurate but much costlier than GGA or meta-GGA methods, so users should compare functional classes, test different Fock-exchange amounts in critical cases, and use proven multi-level or composite approaches for large systems.
The atomic-orbital basis set is another major part of the speed-versus-accuracy compromise.
In principle, basis-set choice is technical because a calculation could be converged all the way to the complete-basis-set limit, where basis-set error disappears.
In real work, finite basis sets are almost always used, so some basis-set error remains.
DFT is usually less sensitive to basis-set errors than correlated wavefunction methods.
That weaker dependence on basis-set size is one practical advantage of DFT.
Still, even an excellent functional will give poor results if the basis set is too small.
The key basis-set property is completeness, commonly described as basis-set size.
Completeness reflects how many mathematical functions are available to describe each electron.
If the function space is too small, basis set incompleteness error occurs because the orbital expansion cannot represent the true electron density well enough.
Put simply, BSIE means the basis set is not flexible enough to describe fine details in the electron density.
Valence electrons usually matter most, so basis sets are categorized by a cardinal number giving how many independent functions describe each occupied valence orbital.
That size is called double-zeta, triple-zeta, quadruple-zeta, and so on, where zeta counts independent atomic functions for each occupied valence orbital.
A second small-basis error is basis set superposition error: nearby atoms or fragments borrow each other's basis functions, which falsely stabilizes compact structures and weak complexes.
This is another extraction artifact from the journal header.
The previous sentence continues here: BSSE can be as large as the interaction energy for noncovalently bound intermolecular complexes.
BSSE is not limited to weak intermolecular binding; small basis sets can also distort conformer energies and even optimized molecular structures.
Therefore, users need to know which tasks are sensitive to basis-set size and when a larger basis set is worth the extra cost.
Section 3 and Figure 5 focus on that decision.

For separated fragments, the counterpoise correction can address BSSE, while cheaper approximate corrections such as gCP or adapted effective core potentials can also correct intramolecular BSSE and are easier to apply.
Approximate counterpoise corrections can fix the worst BSSE problems, for example during geometry optimization with small basis sets.
Using them is about as simple as applying a dispersion correction.
The authors recommend Kruse and Grimme's gCP correction for small-basis geometry optimizations with HF or DFT across many basis sets.

For accurate noncovalent interaction energies, they also recommend Witte and Head-Gordon's DFT-C approach for DFT/def2-SVPD, while warning that minimal basis sets should generally be avoided because their errors become uncontrollable.
Common contracted Gaussian basis-set families include Pople, Dunning, Jensen, and Ahlrichs basis sets.

For routine DFT, the authors do not recommend older Pople or Dunning sets such as 6-31G* or cc-pVXZ because Ahlrichs def2 basis sets are usually more efficient and available for more elements; extra polarization or diffuse functions can be added when anions, dipoles, or polarizabilities need more flexibility.
For example, def2-TZVP can be enlarged with more polarization functions as def2-TZVPP or with diffuse functions as def2-TZVPD or maTZVP.
The paper calls basis sets with extra diffuse or polarization functions large, while reduced def2 variants used in composite methods are called small.
To check basis-set convergence, compare results after increasing the cardinal number, such as def2-TZVP to def2-QZVP, and after adding or removing polarization functions.
For heavy elements, def2-XVP basis sets use matching Stuttgart-Cologne effective core potentials for elements beyond krypton to replace inner core electrons.
That replacement lowers cost and improves accuracy by including scalar relativistic effects, which mainly affect core electrons.
For most thermochemical properties, robust small-core effective core potentials are sufficient.
For properties of very heavy nuclei or core-electron properties such as NMR shieldings, all-electron relativistic calculations with Hamiltonians such as X2C, ZORA, or DKH may be needed.
Some approaches, including X2C, have specially adapted Ahlrichs basis sets, but detailed relativistic methods are outside this paper's scope.

The basis-set recommendations are to watch for BSIE and BSSE, use at least about triple-zeta size for energy properties, add polarization or diffuse functions when needed, test convergence, and use effective core potentials for atoms heavier than krypton.
A final major comparison issue is finite-temperature effects.
Under the Born-Oppenheimer approximation, many calculations describe a molecule at 0 K in its nuclear equilibrium geometry.
Because real experiments happen at finite temperature, optimized structures omit effects such as zero-point vibration, thermal bond stretching, and entropy.
Therefore, a perfect match between a finite-temperature experimental structure and a 0 K optimized structure is not always expected or even desirable.
For ordinary covalent bonds between common atoms, those temperature effects are usually small.
For energies, bare electronic energies from DFT cannot be compared directly with measured reaction enthalpies or free energies.
Even gas-phase measurements near 0 K include zero-point vibrational energy, which is part of every molecule's internal energy.
When a reaction forms or breaks bonds, zero-point energy can change by several kcal mol-1, so it can be important.
At room temperature, additional thermal corrections from vibration, translation, and rotation must be added to obtain internal energy at 298 K.
To move from internal energy to enthalpy, programs usually add pressure-volume work, and to get Gibbs free energy they also include entropy through G = H - TS.
When the number of separate particles changes, entropy can shift free energies by tens of kcal mol-1, and solution reactions also need solvent-model and solvation-free-energy corrections.
Solvation also changes the standard state, usually from gas at 1 atm to solution at 1 mol L-1.
With common default solvent models, users must add a standard-state free-energy correction of 1.89 kcal mol-1 per species at 298 K, in addition to the enthalpy pressure-volume term.
This correction generally matters for solution reactions that are not unimolecular.

Other gas or condensed-phase reference states are possible, so users must examine the solvation model carefully; overall, model the experiment closely, add zero-point and thermal corrections when needed, and use robust entropy models such as mRRHO for large flexible systems.
Because computational chemistry uses many methods based on different physical ideas, the authors recommend further reading on both general and specialized topics.

The extracted text begins a software overview: AMS is noted for Slater-type orbital basis sets up to quadruple-zeta quality, relativistic treatments, a broad toolbox, and a strong graphical interface.
Most quantum-chemistry packages provide the same core DFT abilities; the practical differences are usability, speed, and whether they are free or commercial.
Many advanced programs can use functional libraries such as LibXC or XCfun, greatly expanding the number of available density functionals.
In some programs, native implementations still offer extra technical capabilities beyond those libraries.
The paper then lists common molecular quantum-chemistry packages and briefly highlights their strengths.
These are programs the authors' group has found reliable across many quantum-chemistry applications.

The authors mention many additional packages, then summarize strengths of TURBOMOLE, ORCA, Q-Chem, Psi4, Molpro, and others, including fast DFT implementations, spectroscopy tools, scripting, noncovalent-interaction methods, and wavefunction-in-DFT capabilities.
Some semi-empirical features exist inside large packages, but certain capabilities are only available in the original specialized codes.
The main semi-empirical programs are xtb, DFTB+, and MOPAC, and methods such as single-point Hessian can use native semi-empirical implementations.
The xtb program can also drive other software, for example combining xtb functionality with ORCA for DFT-based single-point Hessian workflows.
Reference [163] gives detailed documentation.
A central practical challenge is balancing computational cost against accuracy and robustness.
To do that, users need to know each method's strengths and weaknesses, the system size, and which property they are trying to compute.
The main systematic errors are self-interaction error, BSSE, BSIE, and missing London dispersion.
How much those errors matter depends strongly on both the molecule and the task.
It is therefore useful to discuss method choices by the common steps in a computational study, because each step targets a different property.
This section aims to help users choose a method set and adapt it to the job.
In plain terms, the authors want to show where one can safely save effort and where one cannot.

A typical multi-level workflow uses cheap GGA, meta-GGA, or composite methods for geometry and frequency calculations, then computes final single-point energies with a higher-level hybrid or double-hybrid method and a larger basis set.
The idea is that GGA or meta-GGA energies have weaknesses such as self-interaction error, but the structures they optimize are often good enough at much lower cost.
Often, even for systems prone to self-interaction error, a hybrid single-point energy on a cheaper optimized structure is as accurate as fully optimizing at the hybrid level.
The paper illustrates this with an anthracene-cyclopropenyl cation potential-energy surface from the r2SCAN-3c work shown in Figure 4.
Although the meta-GGA composite method overestimates the interaction energy, it predicts the equilibrium distance very close to the high-level coupled-cluster reference.

More generally, multi-level workflows rely on systematic differences: cheap structures may not match reference structures perfectly, but their deviations from high-level structures tend to be consistent.
Because the deviations are consistent, energy differences between reactants and products often cancel reliably.

Since that cancellation can involve large energies, every optimization detail - functional, basis set, convergence criteria, program, and technical settings - must be kept consistent across all species.
The cheaper method still needs to describe the system reasonably well.
If the low-level structures are fundamentally wrong because dispersion is missing, the basis set is too tiny, or fragments have the wrong spin or charge state, error cancellation will fail.
The authors distinguish passive error cancellation from active error correction, such as adding D4 for dispersion or gCP for BSSE.
Because structures are usually less sensitive to theory level than energies are, multi-level methods can save a great deal of computation without much loss of accuracy.
Figure 5 summarizes recommended functional and basis-set combinations for typical computational tasks.
The figure uses blue for the best cost-versus-robustness choices, green for accurate robust choices that may be expensive, and red for methods to avoid.
Yellow marks intermediate cases that are not clearly good or bad.
Yellow methods can work, but they are less dependable than green choices.
When using yellow-level methods, users should check the listed systematic errors and often prefer the blue alternatives.
The table also names the main systematic errors and drawbacks the authors expect.
The following sections discuss the table task by task and explain the choices.
For clarity, the authors separate theoretical tasks that are often combined in real workflows.
For example, their discussion of conformational energies means electronic energies only, while vibrational and entropy terms are treated elsewhere.
Most quantum-chemistry projects begin with geometry optimization, either from an experimental structure or from a lower-level guess such as a force-field or semi-empirical structure.
At this stage, the most common mistakes are bad starting structures or wrong charge, spin, or protonation states, which can change results far more than the exact theoretical model.
Therefore, the authors recommend checking the input carefully before launching expensive calculations.
Self-consistent-field convergence problems often signal that something is wrong with the input.
For structures, triple-zeta def2-TZVP is usually enough, and quadruple-zeta basis sets generally waste resources.
Well-balanced double-zeta basis sets can also be useful when paired with gCP and dispersion corrections to reduce BSSE-related structural errors.
Composite methods such as PBEh-3c and r2SCAN-3c combine compact basis sets with gCP and D3 or D4 corrections, making them well suited and strongly recommended for geometry optimization.
For standard thermochemical studies, the authors rarely see a need to optimize structures beyond that level.
For the functional, GGA or meta-GGA methods such as r2SCAN-D4, TPSS-D4, or even PBE-D4 are usually sufficient.
They also benefit strongly from density fitting, making them much cheaper than hybrid functionals.
That means GGA or meta-GGA methods can often use larger basis sets at the same or lower cost than hybrids with small basis sets such as B3LYP/6-31G*.

The authors recommend hybrid functionals for geometry only when there is a clear reason, such as strong self-interaction error, transition-state searches, weakly bound anions, or heavy main-group elements.
A practical first check is to compare r2SCAN-3c with PBEh-3c.
PBE0-D4 with a triple-zeta basis is a very robust but more expensive benchmark-level choice for geometry optimization.
B3LYP-D4 with a triple-zeta basis can be similarly accurate, but the authors find it less robust than PBE0-D4, especially for transition-metal systems.

Range-separated hybrids and double hybrids can also give excellent structures, but double hybrids are usually unnecessary for routine geometry optimization because structural errors often cancel well.
Even very large systems should not be optimized with anything smaller than a polarized double-zeta basis.
The functional should be at least GGA or meta-GGA quality, such as PBE or TPSS, and should always include a dispersion correction such as D3, D4, or VV10.
The authors do not recommend BLYP because it overestimates covalent bond lengths more strongly than the recommended GGA choices.
Vibrational frequency calculations are needed to obtain zero-point energies and thermal corrections to enthalpy and free energy.
They are also used to predict IR and Raman spectra, although spectra are not the focus here.
Because second-derivative calculations become expensive for large systems, users should run them at the cheapest level that still gives reasonable results.
The authors recommend efficient composite methods, or GGA/meta-GGA functionals with a double-zeta basis plus gCP correction.
As with structures, error cancellation is stable, and thermal corrections are usually smaller than the electronic DFT energy, so using a lower level has limited impact.
In many cases, semi-empirical methods such as GFN2-xTB are enough for thermal corrections, as example 4.2 shows.

Low-level semi-empirical methods should be checked carefully for unusual bonds, transition metals, or heavy main-group elements; also, frequencies must be calculated at a geometry that is a true minimum at that same level.
Otherwise, many artificial imaginary frequencies appear and make thermal corrections unreliable.
Therefore, use the same level for geometry optimization and frequency calculation, or optimize a second structure at the cheaper frequency level.

The single-point Hessian method can estimate frequencies for non-equilibrium structures by adding a biasing potential, and it can be used with xTB or DFT workflows; a few tiny imaginary frequencies in large systems are often numerical artifacts from the DFT integration grid.
Tiny imaginary frequencies can be handled by converting them to real frequencies and treating them normally, as in xTB's mRRHO approach, while another common option is to delete low imaginary modes.
Deleting modes can change the number of degrees of freedom inconsistently and may create sizable entropy errors.
If a fully optimized structure has many imaginary modes or any large ones above about 100 cm-1, it is probably not a true minimum and should be distorted and reoptimized.
If the goal is accurate individual frequencies or IR/Raman intensities, rather than just thermal corrections, a hybrid functional and larger basis set can improve spectral intensities.
B3LYP and the cheaper dispersion-corrected B3LYP-3c have a strong track record for IR spectra.
For a broader comparison of methods and Fock-exchange effects on vibrational frequencies, the authors point to Radom and co-workers.
For conformers, reaction energies, barriers, and noncovalent interactions, electronic energy is central, so larger basis sets are needed than for structures or frequencies.
Double-zeta basis sets such as 6-31G** or def2-SVP are not enough for these energy tasks unless they are part of a purpose-built composite method.
Even with counterpoise or gCP corrections, double-zeta residual BSSE and BSIE can still be substantial.
The authors generally recommend at least triple-zeta basis sets because they are often close enough to the basis-set limit.
Users should still test representative cases against quadruple-zeta or a larger triple-zeta basis to check convergence.
Before shrinking the basis set too far, it is usually better to switch from hybrids to cheaper meta-GGA or composite methods designed for smaller basis sets.
Double hybrids are more demanding because their MP2-like part depends more strongly on basis-set size than the DFT part does.
For double hybrids, extrapolating from triple-zeta to quadruple-zeta toward the complete-basis-set limit may be useful, although details are outside the paper.
For consistency, the authors suggest using one method combination for related energy properties in a reaction network, such as omegaB97M-V/QZ single-point energies on r2SCAN-3c structures.
The final choice therefore has to balance all requirements discussed in the next sections.
Conformational energies are electronic energy differences among different shapes of the same molecule with the same covalent connectivity.
Because those structures are similar, conformer energies benefit strongly from error cancellation.
However, conformer energies must be very precise, about 0.1-0.2 kcal mol-1, to predict room-temperature Boltzmann populations well.
That precision matters because different populated conformers can have very different properties, as example 4.3 shows.
For conformers, use high numerical precision and at least triple-zeta basis sets; PBEh-3c is not recommended because it is based on a double-zeta basis.
Conformer energies are especially sensitive to medium- and long-range electron correlation, so dispersion correction is crucial.
For metal-organic systems, density- or charge-dependent corrections such as VV10 or D4 are preferred over charge-independent D3.
Benchmark conformer subsets suggest r2SCAN-D4, r2SCAN-3c, and B97-3c are especially good for accurate conformer energies.
For small systems where cost is manageable, hybrid functionals can also be used.
The authors recommend omegaB97X-V and omegaB97M-V, or their D4/D3 analogues; B3LYP with dispersion and a suitable basis can work, but r2SCAN-3c recently outperformed it at much lower cost.
Double hybrids are appropriate for small systems when maximum accuracy and robustness are needed.
The best GMTKN55 performers include omegaB97M(2) and revDSD-PBEP86-D4.

The authors also recommend the widely available PWPB95-D4 double hybrid, while warning that semi-empirical and force-field methods are unreliable for final conformer energies and should only be used early with broad energy windows followed by DFT re-ranking.
Reaction energies compare the total electronic energies of reactants, products, and intermediates that are minima on the potential-energy surface.
Because reactants and products can differ more in shape and electronic structure, reaction energies benefit less from error cancellation than conformer energies do.
The acceptable error for reaction energies is also usually larger.
An error of about 1-2 kcal mol-1 is often good enough and can be difficult even for experiment.
Although details depend on the reaction, reaction energies generally need large triple-zeta or quadruple-zeta basis sets for convergence.
Basis-set dependence should be checked by comparing single-point energies with the next smaller basis, such as def2-QZVP versus def2-TZVP.
Even under tight computational limits, users should not go below triple-zeta quality for reaction energies.
If cost is too high and self-interaction error is not central, switch to a semi-local meta-GGA rather than shrinking the basis set below triple-zeta.
Surprisingly, r2SCAN-3c is very efficient and performs better for GMTKN55 reaction energies than B3LYP-D4/QZ and PW6B95/QZ hybrids.
For nonmetal systems, adding some Fock exchange usually improves the accuracy and robustness of reaction energies.
For global hybrids, about 25% Fock exchange is often optimal for reaction energies.
Barrier heights often need even more Fock exchange, sometimes around 50%, depending on reaction type.
If the same functional must handle both reaction energies and barriers, a global hybrid requires a compromise.
The authors recommend PW6B95 with D3 or D4 correction because it gives accurate, robust thermochemistry and has been their default hybrid for mechanistic studies.
Range-separated functionals such as omegaB97X-V and omegaB97M-V help resolve the conflict between low Fock exchange for reaction energies and high Fock exchange for barriers.
Because their exchange fraction changes with distance, these functionals can perform well for both reaction energies and barrier heights.
On GMTKN55, those two range-separated hybrids are the only hybrid functionals with WTMAD2 below 4 kcal mol-1.
Double hybrids can be even more accurate and robust, but they are the most expensive and need larger basis sets.
The ideal amount of Fock exchange also depends on the reaction class.
The values above mainly apply to main-group chemistry; transition-metal compounds usually need lower Fock exchange, roughly half as much.
Therefore, very high-exchange and range-separated functionals should be used cautiously for transition-metal systems.
Examples such as M06-2X and PBEh-3c have shown larger errors in recent transition-metal reaction benchmarks.
Barrier heights are electronic energy differences between a transition state and the relevant reactants or products.
Transition states often contain stretched bonds and near-degenerate orbitals with weakly bound electrons, making the electronic structure difficult.
Because of that, transition states are prone to self-interaction error, which makes GGA and meta-GGA functionals underestimate transition-state energies and barriers.
The size of this error depends on how much one-electron character the breaking bond has in the transition state.
Hydrogen-transfer and bond-dissociation reactions tend to show the largest errors.
Heavy-atom pericyclic reactions show smaller errors, and inversion or conformational processes that keep covalent bonds intact show almost none.

Cheap GGA or meta-GGA methods can be used for special barriers such as conformational or inversion barriers after testing, but generally the authors advise against semi-local functionals for barrier heights.
Because barrier heights are especially challenging, Section 4.4 gives an extended example.
Range-separated hybrids are strongly recommended for barriers because they reduce self-interaction-related errors.
omegaB97M-V and omegaB97X-V perform particularly well on GMTKN55 barrier-height subsets and other benchmarks.
If a global hybrid must be used, choose one with more than 30% Fock exchange and compare it against a range-separated hybrid.
Barrier-focused global hybrids such as BMK and MPWB1K typically use about 40-50% Fock exchange.
That much Fock exchange usually worsens reaction-energy performance.
Double hybrids are also well suited for barriers because they use high Fock exchange while still giving accurate reaction energies.
Transition states can also converge more slowly with basis-set size, so quadruple-zeta basis sets should be considered for barrier heights.
London dispersion can strongly affect reaction energies and barriers, especially for molecules larger than about 20-30 atoms, so it should be included explicitly.
Finding the transition-state structure is itself an important part of reaction-barrier work.
Although program-specific transition-state search details are outside the paper, the same method-choice ideas apply to surface scans and related searches.
Transition-state searches require many energy and gradient evaluations, so lower-level methods are often unavoidable for the search stage.

Users can use hybrid composite methods with double-zeta basis sets, such as PBEh-3c, or carefully use semi-empirical methods to get transition-state guesses, then refine them at the recommended level.
For detailed transition-state search procedures, the authors point to software manuals such as the ORCA manual.
Noncovalent interaction energies compare a bound complex with its separated molecular fragments.
Because no covalent bonds change when fragments bind, noncovalent interaction energies benefit very strongly from error cancellation.
At the same time, these energies are often small for each atom pair.
That small size means high numerical precision is often needed, sometimes more than for reaction energies.
BSSE and self-interaction error can be similar in size to the noncovalent energy itself, so their influence must be checked.
London dispersion is usually a major part of noncovalent binding, making dispersion corrections especially important.

VV10 may help for unusual charged systems because it depends on density, but D3-ATM and D4 are often more accurate for highly polarizable small-gap systems because of better C6 coefficients and three-body dispersion terms.
For efficient noncovalent interaction calculations, the authors recommend 3c composite methods, especially r2SCAN-3c for most systems.
PBEh-3c can be better for highly polar systems where self-interaction error is more serious.
However, PBEh-3c's small basis can create BSSE and BSIE problems in those same cases.
Head-Gordon's DFT-C approach combines B97M-V with def2-SVPD and a tailored gCP-style correction, giving noncovalent interaction energies close to B97M-V/QZ in tests.
Section 4.2 demonstrates that DFT-C approach for noncovalent interactions.
Because error cancellation is strong for noncovalent energies, semi-empirical methods such as GFN2-xTB or PM6-D3H4 can sometimes give reasonable structures and interactions at much lower cost.
For very large complexes, a good strategy is to use very cheap methods for structures and frequencies, then compute final energies with composite DFT.
At the high-accuracy end, omegaB97M-V and omegaB97X-V give excellent noncovalent interaction energies with triple-zeta or quadruple-zeta basis sets.
Double hybrids are also accurate and robust for noncovalent interactions, but they are more basis-set sensitive and expensive.

Noncovalent interactions involving very small-gap systems such as metals need special treatment; the examples in this section were chosen to illustrate the earlier arguments about methods, basis sets, and multi-level workflows.
The examples also show that one case is never as informative as a broad, well-designed benchmark.
A low-level method may occasionally work surprisingly well, while a high-level method may miss by several kcal mol-1 in a specific case.
Such outliers are expected in real work because DFT and other efficient methods are approximate.
The authors repeat their main point: best-practice DFT is not about getting every number perfect to the final kcal mol-1.
The goal is to avoid large systematic errors by choosing robust protocols suited to the actual physical system.
Figure 6 examines a classic organic transformation: dimerizing 3,6-dimethylidenecyclohexa-1,4-diene to [2.2]paracyclophane and then isomerizing it to [2.2]metacyclophane.
Experimental heats of formation can give reaction enthalpies, but the dimerization value has a large uncertainty because the monomer's heat of formation is uncertain: 41.9 +/- 10.6 kcal mol-1.
The isomerization uncertainty is smaller, giving 17.9 +/- 2.9 kcal mol-1 after rounding.
To get sharper reference values, the authors computed high-level W1-F12 coupled-cluster electronic energies and combined them with PBE0-D4/def2-TZVP zero-point and enthalpy corrections.
Because W1-F12 has a much smaller estimated error than the experiment, the authors use it as the reference.
They used geometries computed consistently at the same DFT level throughout.
The calculated reference enthalpies are 48.7 and 19.0 kcal mol-1, both inside the experimental error bars.

The dimerization is difficult computationally because it forms two strained single bonds and the product has strong intramolecular dispersion and exchange-correlation interactions between close aromatic rings.
Plain B3LYP without dispersion badly underestimates the dimerization enthalpy, giving 25.2 kcal mol-1 at quadruple-zeta quality.
Adding D4 dispersion greatly improves B3LYP, raising the value to 43.4 kcal mol-1.
Even then, B3LYP-D4 remains 5.1 kcal mol-1 away from the W1-F12 reference.
The PWPB95-D4/def2-QZVP double hybrid matches the reference better.
It gives 48.5 kcal mol-1, almost identical to the high-level W1-F12 value.
The isomerization is less sensitive to functional errors because the two isomers are chemically similar, so their errors cancel.
As a result, all tested DFT methods are reasonably close, and PWPB95-D4/QZ again matches W1-F12 extremely well.
For these reactions, enthalpy corrections are small compared with electronic-energy differences; the source sentence then accidentally merges into the next noncovalent-binding discussion.
For binding free energies, several large contributions must each be accurate because they only partially cancel to produce the small experimental ΔG values, often around 1-15 kcal mol-1.
The source text is truncated here; it is introducing the example complex discussed next.
Noncovalent interactions are central in biological and supramolecular chemistry, so predicting binding free energies for such complexes is important.
This is hard because chemically relevant host-guest systems are often large and flexible.
To compare with solution-phase binding experiments, thermal and entropy terms must be included.
This is especially important for bimolecular binding reactions.
The standard binding free-energy expression combines gas-phase electronic energy, solvation free energy, and thermostatistical contributions from the mRRHO model.

The example is a charged macrocyclic host, CBPQT4+, binding bromobenzene in water with an experimental ΔG of about 5 kcal mol-1; the first challenge is modeling the finite-temperature solution structure realistically.
The authors handle that by searching conformers with CREST and CENSO.
They previously showed that GFN2-xTB with the ALPB implicit solvation model is efficient and suitable for the initial conformer search.
For this fairly rigid system, the main goal is to find the lowest-energy conformer, which is usually enough.
For more flexible hosts or guests, users should consider other low-energy conformers too; even this relatively rigid example has twelve conformers within 2.5 kcal mol-1.
Other conformers matter when binding strongly changes the accessible shapes, such as when a free alkyl chain becomes locked in the complex.
The authors leave that more detailed conformer issue to references [67] and [214].
For final geometry optimization and final energy calculation of the best conformer, the authors recommend r2SCAN-3c with DCOSMO-RS implicit solvation.
SMD, CPCM, and COSMO can also be used, in that order of preference.

The source sentence is partially broken, but its main point is that outdated popular methods are not justified and that solvation free energy is the hardest part of the workflow, especially for charged systems in polar hydrogen-bonding solvents.
A 2-3 kcal mol-1 solvation error is realistic because ΔδGsolv is large and even the best implicit solvent models have about 10-20% intrinsic uncertainty.
In the authors' experience, COSMO-RS is the most reliable implicit model for this, with SMD as a useful alternative.
Purely electrostatic models such as COSMO and CPCM often do worse because they miss non-electrostatic solvent terms, especially when solvent-accessible surface area changes.
Despite large individual errors, the standard workflow often predicts binding free energies within 1-3 kcal mol-1 because errors cancel fortuitously.
For this example, many method combinations give a calculated ΔG of 5.4 kcal mol-1, close to the experimental value.
The workflow is also fast: the shown example takes about 30 hours on a typical eight-core CPU.
That speed comes from semi-empirical methods for frequencies and efficient meta-GGA composite DFT for structures and single-point energies.
With this multi-level approach, useful affinity predictions are feasible for complexes of roughly 200-300 atoms.
The Figure 7 workflow is automated in CREST and CENSO and can be run with two simple UNIX commands.
Relative electronic energies can strongly affect property predictions when properties are averaged over molecular ensembles.
The optical-rotation example for alpha- and beta-D-glucopyranose shows this because optical rotation depends strongly on structure and on correctly weighting populated conformers.
The alpha and beta isomers have very different optical rotations, so the equilibrium between them must be described accurately.
Because this problem requires many protocol decisions, the authors use it to demonstrate the Figure 2 flowchart.
First, these sugars are normal organic molecules with no expected multireference character.
Therefore, the authors use single-reference DFT, plus semi-empirical methods for the first conformer search.
Second, because optical rotation was measured in solution, every step includes a solvation model.
Technical limitations lead them to use DCOSMO-RS for geometry optimization, COSMO for optical rotation, and COSMO-RS for free-energy evaluation.
COSMO-RS improves free energies but cannot be used for geometry optimizations.
Therefore, the authors use DCOSMO-RS, an improved COSMO variant, during geometry optimization.
Because DCOSMO-RS and COSMO-RS cannot calculate optical rotation directly, the simpler COSMO model is used for that property calculation.
The third issue is conformers and molecular flexibility.
This matters strongly here because both alpha- and beta-D-glucopyranose are flexible due to their many hydroxy groups.
The authors therefore use a conformer search to find the low-energy shapes that contribute to the result.
They use CREST together with CENSO.
CREST quickly finds low-lying conformers with a semi-empirical method, and CENSO then refines their structures and energies with more accurate DFT.
The next choice is the functional.
As discussed in Section 3.4, r2SCAN-3c gives good geometries and conformational free energies.
So the authors choose r2SCAN-3c as the default method here.
They also test and discuss alternatives below.
No relativistic treatment is needed because the molecules contain no heavy elements.
The basis set is already fixed because a 3c composite method includes its built-in def2-mTZVPP basis.
The final ensemble protocol uses r2SCAN-3c with DCOSMO-RS for geometries and r2SCAN-3c with COSMO-RS for energy ranking.
Each anomer has 15-18 relevant low-energy conformers, and all are included in the Boltzmann average.
The property being averaged is specific optical rotation, which requires special computational settings.
For optical rotation itself, PBE with the diffuse double-zeta def2-SVPD basis and COSMO solvation is sufficient.
Hybrids are usually recommended for optical rotation, but PBE was chosen here for technical reasons after comparison with B3LYP.
A double-zeta basis is enough because optical rotation is not very sensitive to basis-set size.
Diffuse functions are important for reliable optical rotation, and although the protocol has many decisions, the most visible one is which functional ranks the electronic energies.
The authors next examine how that functional choice changes the final Boltzmann-weighted optical rotation.
They re-rank the conformer ensembles using B3LYP variants, B3LYP with and without D4 and with 6-31G*, plus BP86-D4/QZ for the electronic energy contribution.
All other terms, including zero-point, thermal, and solvation contributions, come from the earlier r2SCAN-3c calculation.
Consistent with benchmarks, r2SCAN-3c ranks conformers well and gives a Boltzmann-weighted optical rotation close to the experimental value of 52.7 in standard optical-rotation units.
B3LYP-D4/QZ ranking also works well, giving 54.6.
Plain B3LYP/QZ gives a much worse value of 41.2, showing that dispersion correction matters even for fairly small molecules.
B3LYP/6-31G* depends on an uncontrolled cancellation between missing dispersion and BSSE.
Even with that cancellation, it overweights alpha-D-glucopyranose and gives a poor optical rotation of 61.7.
BP86-D4/QZ performs even worse for this conformer-sensitive property, giving 72.2.
The example shows that good results require including all relevant effects, especially London dispersion and basis-set completeness.
It also shows that properties sensitive to conformers, such as optical rotation, can test whether theoretical approximations are good enough.
Reliable reaction-barrier calculations are essential for studying complex reaction mechanisms.
They help explain key reactions and support computer-aided catalyst design and reaction tuning; SN2 and Diels-Alder reactions are classic organic examples.
Even these familiar reaction types require careful quantum-chemical method choices.

Accurate reference activation energies are available for ethylene plus cyclopentadiene and for hydroxide attacking fluoromethane, and the authors keep the original benchmark geometries for fair comparison.
For both examples, BP86-D3/QZ strongly underestimates the barrier.
For the SN2 reaction specifically, it is 10.2 kcal mol-1 too low.
The problem is charge delocalization in the transition state, which creates self-interaction error for the GGA method.
M06-L does better, likely because it is heavily empirical and was trained partly on similar barrier-height cases.
Still, hybrids such as PBE0 are expected to improve on GGA and meta-GGA methods because they describe the physics better.
As expected, PBE0-D3/QZ improves substantially over BP86-D3/QZ, and M06-2X improves over M06-L.
For M06-2X, the very high Fock-exchange fraction helps this case by reducing self-interaction error.
But very high-exchange functionals should still be used cautiously.
Section 2.5 explains that caution.
Range-separated hybrids such as omegaB97X-V/QZ and omegaB97M-D4/QZ perform well for both reactions; omegaB97X-V is only 1.8 kcal mol-1 low for the SN2 barrier.
It also matches the Diels-Alder barrier well, overestimating by only 1.8 kcal mol-1.
Figure 11 gives another hard example: hydrogen-atom transfer.
In this reaction, a hydrogen atom moves from a molybdenum hydride complex to a Gomberg-type radical, and experiment gives an activation free energy of 19.2 kcal mol-1.
As discussed earlier, stretched-bond transition states are vulnerable to self-interaction error, so GGA and meta-GGA methods are expected to underestimate the barrier.
This is especially true for hydrogen-transfer bonds because they often have strong one-electron character.
Unlike the previous electronic-energy examples, the target here is a solution activation free energy, so zero-point, thermal, solvation, and entropy terms also matter.
Although GGA and meta-GGA methods should be treated cautiously for transition-state electronic energies, they often still give reasonable geometries and thermal corrections.
Therefore, the authors use B97-3c for geometry optimization and frequency calculation in this example.
The source sentence is truncated at a method label, beginning a comparison of BP86-D4/QZ for the electronic energy.
Continuing the hydrogen-transfer example, BP86-D4/QZ underestimates the activation free energy by almost 4 kcal mol-1.
The underestimate gets worse with smaller basis sets, reaching more than 7 kcal mol-1 for BP86-D4 with a double-zeta basis.
B3LYP-D4 slightly overestimates the activation free energy by 1.4 kcal mol-1, close to the usual 1 kcal mol-1 chemical-accuracy target.
Even for hybrids, small basis sets underestimate the barrier in this sensitive case.
The GGA calculation gives a negative electronic activation energy and the transition state is not a stationary point on that potential-energy surface.
B3LYP-D4/QZ gives a small but reasonable electronic activation energy of 3.0 kcal mol-1.
That small electronic barrier shows why solvation, enthalpy, and free-energy corrections are essential for comparison with experiment.
Including all contributions raises the barrier from 3.0 kcal mol-1 electronically to 7.2 kcal mol-1 as enthalpy and finally 20.6 kcal mol-1 as free energy, close to experiment.
The authors view the last 20-30 years of quantum chemistry, especially DFT, as a major success.
It is remarkable that non-specialists can now run useful quantum-chemistry calculations for large chemical systems on desktop computers.
Widely available computational tools have already strongly influenced chemistry, and their impact is likely to grow.
The authors hope their recommendations make everyday DFT predictions more reliable for many chemists.
They emphasize choosing the right compromise between speed and accuracy while still getting the right answer for the right physical reason.
Fast methods can actually improve reliability by letting researchers explore conformers, dynamics, and explicit solvation more thoroughly.
In the authors' experience, missing important low-energy conformers can cause larger errors than the functional or basis-set approximation itself.
They encourage systematic conformer searches, molecular dynamics, more routine explicit-solvent treatments as methods improve, and efficient multi-level workflows for realistic models.
Electronic-structure theory still has unresolved challenges.
Standard DFT handles many important problems, but open-shell transition metals, highly charged strongly solvated systems, and entropy remain difficult.
Those cases often require non-standard methods and expert input.
The authors think peak accuracy of basic density functionals has begun to saturate after strong progress over the previous decade.

Promising directions include local hybrids, non-self-consistent treatments using cheap GGA or meta-GGA densities to avoid potential-driven errors, and early machine-learned functionals, though these are empirical and not necessarily cheap.

DFT is also the method used to generate huge molecular databases for machine learning, and the authors see room to make the best functionals much faster without losing robustness or numerical accuracy.
Key speed targets are better approximations for nonlocal Fock exchange in hybrid and double-hybrid functionals and faster numerical integration for GGA and meta-GGA exchange-correlation energy.

Because DFT is a general first-principles approach, the authors believe many conclusions also apply to molecular solids and liquids under periodic boundary conditions, even though those are not treated here.
Special low-band-gap cases such as metallic solids may need extra technical settings to prevent the approximations from breaking down.
